column6: or attackers identification

column14: and found that several of them can be used as basic blocks for fingerprinting. Using this technique

column16: to distinguish among a perfectly identical (both in software and hardware) set of computers. CryptoFP was able to correctly discriminate all the identical computers in this scenario and recognize the same computer also under different CPU load configurations
column17: outperforming every other hardware fingerprinting method. We then show how CryptoFP can be implemented using a combination of the HTML5 Cryptography API and standard timing API for web device fingerprinting. In this case

column6: Dan;2019;CCS '19;Conference;AdVersarial: Perceptual Ad Blocking meets Adversarial Machine Learning;Perceptual ad-blocking is a novel approach that detects online advertisements based on their visual content. Compared to traditional filter lists

column10: Andriy;2020;CCS '20;Conference;TrafficSliver: Fighting Website Fingerprinting Attacks with Traffic Splitting;Website fingerprinting (WFP) aims to infer information about the content of encrypted and anonymized connections by observing patterns of data flows based on the size and direction of packets. By collecting traffic traces at a malicious Tor entry node — one of the weakest adversaries in the attacker model of Tor — a passive eavesdropper can leverage the captured meta-data to reveal the websites visited by a Tor user. As recently shown
column11: WFP is significantly more effective and realistic than assumed. Concurrently
column12: former WFP defenses are either infeasible for deployment in real-world settings or defend against specific WFP attacks only.To limit the exposure of Tor users to WFP
column13: we propose novel lightweight WFP defenses
column14: TrafficSliver
column15: which successfully counter today's WFP classifiers with reasonable bandwidth and latency overheads and
column16: thus
column17: make them attractive candidates for adoption in Tor. Through user-controlled splitting of traffic over multiple Tor entry nodes
column18: TrafficSliver limits the data a single entry node can observe and distorts repeatable traffic patterns exploited by WFP attacks. We first propose a network-layer defense
column19: in which we apply the concept of multipathing entirely within the Tor network. We show that our network-layer defense reduces the accuracy from more than 98% to less than 16% for all state-of-the-art WFP attacks without adding any artificial delays or dummy traffic. We further suggest an elegant client-side application-layer defense
column20: which is independent of the underlying anonymization network. By sending single HTTP requests for different web objects over distinct Tor entry nodes
column21: our application-layer defense reduces the detection rate of WFP classifiers by almost 50 percentage points. Although it offers lower protection than our network-layer defense
column22: it provides a security boost at the cost of a very low implementation overhead and is fully compatible with today's Tor network.;privacy ─ web privacy ─ anonymous communication ─ onion routing ─ traffic analysis ─ website fingerprinting;;;;;

column2: Timothy;2018;WWW '18;Conference;An Automated Approach to Auditing Disclosure of Third-Party Data Collection in Website Privacy Policies;"A dominant regulatory model for web privacy is ""notice and choice"". In this model

column2: WWW 2021;Conference paper;Towards realistic and reproducibleweb crawl measurements;"Accurate web measurement is critical for understanding and improving security and privacy online. column5: have limitations in terms of accuracy and effectiveness. In this work
column6: we propose WTAGRAPH
column7: a web tracking and advertising detection framework based on Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). We first construct an attributed homogenous multi-graph (AHMG) that represents HTTP network traffic
column8: and formulate web tracking and advertising detection as a task of GNN-based edge representation learning and classification in AHMG. We then design four components in WTAGRAPH so that it can (1) collect HTTP network traffic
column9: DOM
column10: and JavaScript data
column11: (2) construct AHMG and extract corresponding edge and node features
column12: (3) build a GNN model for edge representation learning and WTA detection in the transductive learning setting
column13: and (4) use a pre-trained GNN model for WTA detection in the inductive learning setting. We evaluate WTAGRAPH on a dataset collected from Alexa Top 10K websites
column14: and show that WTAGRAPH can effectively detect WTA requests in both transductive and inductive learning settings. Manual verification results indicate that WTAGRAPH can detect new WTA requests that are missed by filter lists and recognize non-WTA requests that are mistakenly labeled by filter lists. Our ablation analysis
column15: evasion evaluation
column16: and real-time evaluation show that WTAGRAPH can have a competitive performance with flexible deployment options in practice.  © 2022 IEEE.;graph neural networks ─ privacy ─ web tracking and advertising;;;;;

column2: WPES 2022;Conference paper;Fingerprinting and Personal Information Leakage from Touchscreen Interactions;The study aims to understand and quantify the privacy threat landscape of touch-based biometrics. column4: we investigated the possibility of predicting personal information attributes through the use of touch interaction behavior. The attributes we investigated were age

column6: dominant hand
column7: country of origin
column8: height
column9: and weight. We found that our model can predict the age group and gender of users with up to 66% and 62% accuracy respectively. Finally
column10: we discuss countermeasures

column2: ADMA 2023;Conference paper;Privacy Lost in Online Education: Analysis of Web Tracking Evolution;Digital tracking poses a significant and multifaceted threat to personal privacy and integrity. Tracking techniques

column2: CHI 2024;Conference paper;Measuring Compliance with the California Consumer Privacy Act Over Space and Time;The widespread sharing of consumers' personal information with third parties raises significant privacy concerns. The California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) mandates that online businesses offer consumers the option to opt out of the sale and sharing of personal information. Our study automatically tracks the presence of the opt-out link longitudinally across multiple states after the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA) went into effect. We categorize websites based on whether they are subject to CCPA and investigate cases of potential non-compliance. We find a number of websites that implement the opt-out link early and across all examined states but also find a significant number of CCPA-subject websites that fail to offer any opt-out methods even when CCPA is in effect. Our findings can shed light on how websites are reacting to the CCPA and identify potential gaps in compliance and opt-out method designs that hinder consumers from exercising CCPA opt-out rights. © 2024 Copyright held by the owner/author(s);CCPA ─ Compliance ─ Opt-out ─ Privacy ─ Web Tracking;;;;;

column3: customer data represents a potential goldmine. Regarding the users' online activity
column4: sophisticated and obscure user-tracking mechanisms of high accuracy have arisen like Browser Fingerprinting
column5: which collects attributes of users' devices to generate a unique identifier token. This threatens the users' privacy. Although modern browsers allow blocking JavaScript to prevent this type of tracking
column6: it also affects the overall users' experience (as websites typically do not work properly without JavaScript). To tackle this issue
column7: we propose a browser extension to anonymize a user's browser information by performing an API normalization against passive and JavaScript-based fingerprinting. This extension masks the actual system values before sending them to the requested website. This causes that the users' token also changes
column8: becomes more challenging for advertisers to track users. Our experimental results are promising
column9: as they have shown that the plugin provides appropriate anonymity to users by making their identity less unique.  © 2023 IEEE.;Browser Fingerprinting ─ Personal Data ─ Tracking ─ Web Privacy;;;;;

column1: Scopus;https://www.scopus.com/inward/record.uri?eid=2-s2.0-85127075488&doi=10.1007%2f978-3-030-96791-8_19&partnerID=40&md5=3d2eca7b539986bdb280781a2082c8fe;10.1007/978-3-030-96791-8_19;Zhang Y. ─ Yang L. ─ Jia J. ─ Ying S. ─ Zhou Y.;2022;4th EAI International Conference on Security and Privacy in New Computing Environments
column2: SPNCE 2021;Conference paper;RAP: A Lightweight Application Layer Defense Against Website Fingerprinting;Website fingerprinting (WFP) attacks threaten user privacy on anonymity networks because they can be used by network surveillants to identify webpages that are visited by users based on extracted features from the network traffic. There are currently defenses to reduce the threat of WFP
column3: but these defense measures have some defects ─ some defenses are too expensive to deploy
column4: and some have been defeated by stronger WFP attack methods. In this work
column5: we propose a lightweight application layer defense method
column6: RAP
column7: which can resist current WFP attacks with very low data and latency overheads ─ more importantly
column8: it is easy to deploy. We randomly deploy important resource files
column9: such as JS and CSS
column10: to multiple Tor OR servers in advance and update them regularly. By randomly scrambling the resource request order
column11: a single request is sent and received through multiple independent paths with different Tor entry ORs. To randomize the traffic distribution
column12: users randomly obtain the website resource files directly from the Tor node server
column13: rather than from the original server
column14: when browsing the website. In this way
column15: the best attack accuracy is reduced from 98% to 53%. Additionally
column16: to confuse the traffic
column17: we request a small amount of additional HTML text instead of the whole website resources
column18: which reduces the effect of state-of-the-art WFP attacks to 40% with 13% data overhead and 31% latency overhead. © 2022
column19: ICST Institute for Computer Sciences

column2: USENIX Security 2024;Conference paper;Pixel Thief: Exploiting SVG Filter Leakage in Firefox and Chrome;Web privacy is challenged by pixel-stealing attacks
column3: which allow attackers to extract content from embedded iframes and to detect visited links. To protect against multiple pixel-stealing attacks that exploited timing variations in SVG filters
column4: browser vendors repeatedly adapted their implementations to eliminate timing variations. In this work we demonstrate that past efforts are still not sufficient. We show how web-based attackers can mount cache-based side-channel attacks to monitor data-dependent memory accesses in filter rendering functions. We identify conditions under which browsers elect the non-default CPU implementation of SVG filters
column5: and develop techniques for achieving access to the high-resolution timers required for cache attacks. We then develop efficient techniques to use the pixel-stealing attack for text recovery from embedded pages and to achieve high-speed history sniffing. To the best of our knowledge
column6: our attack is the first to leak multiple bits per screen refresh
column7: achieving an overall rate of 267 bits per second. © USENIX Security Symposium 2024.All rights reserved.;;;;;;

column3: before
column4: during
column5: and after the lockdown). Our study shows that users unintentionally share more personal identifiable information when writing about the pandemic situation (e.g.

column7: Search Engines

column2: CoNEXT 2022;Conference paper;How gullible are web measurement tools? A case study analysing and strengthening OpenWPM's reliability;Automated browsers are widely used to study the web at scale. Their premise is that they measure what regular browsers would encounter on the web. In practice

column8: in a scan of 100

column2: EuroUSEC 2022;Conference paper;What Cookie Consent Notices Do Users Prefer: A Study In The Wild;Laws like GDPR in the EU mandated all websites operating in their jurisdiction to obtain users' informed consent before tracking those users and collecting their data. Today

column6: not all designs are uniformly liked by the users. Thus the first step of improving cookie consent notice UI design and moving to a better consent mechanism is to understand whether users prefer one design over another in the wild and why. To answer these questions
column7: in this work

column2: CCS 2020;Conference paper;TrafficSliver: Fighting Website Fingerprinting Attacks with Traffic Splitting;Website fingerprinting (WFP) aims to infer information about the content of encrypted and anonymized connections by observing patterns of data flows based on the size and direction of packets. By collecting traffic traces at a malicious Tor entry node - - one of the weakest adversaries in the attacker model of Tor - - a passive eavesdropper can leverage the captured meta-data to reveal the websites visited by a Tor user. As recently shown
column3: WFP is significantly more effective and realistic than assumed. Concurrently
column4: former WFP defenses are either infeasible for deployment in real-world settings or defend against specific WFP attacks only. To limit the exposure of Tor users to WFP
column5: we propose novel lightweight WFP defenses
column6: TrafficSliver
column7: which successfully counter today's WFP classifiers with reasonable bandwidth and latency overheads and
column8: thus
column9: make them attractive candidates for adoption in Tor. Through user-controlled splitting of traffic over multiple Tor entry nodes
column10: TrafficSliver limits the data a single entry node can observe and distorts repeatable traffic patterns exploited by WFP attacks. We first propose a network-layer defense
column11: in which we apply the concept of multipathing entirely within the Tor network. We show that our network-layer defense reduces the accuracy from more than 98% to less than 16% for all state-of-the-art WFP attacks without adding any artificial delays or dummy traffic. We further suggest an elegant client-side application-layer defense
column12: which is independent of the underlying anonymization network. By sending single HTTP requests for different web objects over distinct Tor entry nodes
column13: our application-layer defense reduces the detection rate of WFP classifiers by almost 50 percentage points. Although it offers lower protection than our network-layer defense
column14: it provides a security boost at the cost of a very low implementation overhead and is fully compatible with today's Tor network. © 2020 ACM.;anonymous communication ─ onion routing ─ privacy ─ traffic analysis ─ web privacy ─ website fingerprinting;;;;;

column2: WPES 2023;Conference paper;Unveiling the Impact of User-Agent Reduction and Client Hints: A Measurement Study;The user-agent string contains the details of a user's device

column6: we attempt to characterize the effects of these major changes through a large-scale web measurement on the top 100K websites. Using an instrumented crawler
column7: we quantify access to high-entropy browser features through UA-CH HTTP headers and the JavaScript API. We measure access delegation to third parties and investigate whether the new client hints are already used by tracking

column4: in decentralized web applications content and context factors (called content trust) become critical due to the arbitrary number of potential data providers and the contextual nature of trust. Besides
column5: the dynamic nature of the de-centralized web necessitates trust-aware decisions that are made autonomously by the machine in a collaborative environment without further human intervention. To address these challenges
column6: we present ConTED
column7: a content trust evaluation framework for enabling decentralized Web applications to evaluate content trust autonomously. We also describe the architecture concept
column8: which makes it feasible to integrate content trust models for decentralized Web applications. To demonstrate the feasibility
column9: ConTED is integrated with aTLAS testbed
column10: a web-based test bed to examine trust for a redecentralized web. Finally
column11: we evaluate ConTED in terms of scalability and accuracy through a set of experiments.  © 2022 IEEE.;Decentralization ─ Trust ─ Web application modelling and engineering ─ Web security and privacy;;;;;

Internet Technol.;Article;Breaking Bad: Quantifying the Addiction of Web Elements to JavaScript;While JavaScript established itself as a cornerstone of the modern web
column2: it also constitutes a major tracking and security vector
column3: thus raising critical privacy and security concerns. In this context
column4: some browser extensions propose to systematically block scripts reported by crowdsourced trackers lists. However
column5: this solution heavily depends on the quality of these built-in lists
column6: which may be deprecated or incomplete
column7: thus exposing the visitor to unknown trackers. In this article
column8: we explore a different strategy by investigating the benefits of disabling JavaScript in the browser. More specifically
column9: by adopting such a strict policy
column10: we aim to quantify the JavaScript addiction of web elements composing a web page through the observation of web breakages. As there is no standard mechanism for detecting such breakages
column11: we introduce a framework to inspect several page features when blocking JavaScript
column12: that we deploy to analyze 6
column13: 384 pages
column14: including landing and internal web pages. We discover that 43% of web pages are not strictly dependent on JavaScript and that more than 67% of pages are likely to be usable as long as the visitor only requires the content from the main section of the page
column15: for which the user most likely reached the page
column16: while reducing the number of tracking requests by 85% on average. Finally
column17: we discuss the viability of currently browsing the web without JavaScript and detail multiple incentives for websites to be kept usable without JavaScript.  © 2023 Copyright held by the owner/author(s). Publication rights licensed to ACM.;JavaScript ─ page breakage ─ Web privacy;;;;;

column1: Scopus;https://www.scopus.com/inward/record.uri?eid=2-s2.0-85179838000&doi=10.1145%2f3576915.3623084&partnerID=40&md5=225e6fe1c3cf73a330d310a6a15290a9;10.1145/3576915.3623084;Lee C. ─ Son S.;2023;30th ACM SIGSAC Conference on Computer and Communications Security
column2: CCS 2023;Conference paper;AdCPG: Classifying JavaScript Code Property Graphs with Explanations for Ad and Tracker Blocking;Advertising and tracking service (ATS) blocking has been safeguarding the privacy of millions of Internet users from privacy-invasive tracking behaviors. Previous research has proposed using a graph representation that models the structural relationships in loading web resources and then conducting ATS node classification based on this graph representation. However
column3: these context-based ATS classification methods suffer from (1) inconsistent classification due to the varying context in which ATS resources are loaded and (2) a lack of explainability of the classification results
column4: making it difficult to identify the code-level causes for ATS classification. We propose AdCPG
column5: a graph neural network (GNN) framework tailored for ATS classification. Our approach focuses on classifying JavaScript (JS) content rather than considering the loading context of web resources. Given JS files
column6: AdCPG leverages their code property graphs (CPGs) and conducts graph classification on these CPGs that model the semantic and structural information of these JS files. To provide the explanations for ATS classification
column7: AdCPG highlights the JS code that contributes the most to classifying the JS files into ATS using a GNN explainer. AdCPG achieved an accuracy of 98.75% on the Tranco top-10K websites
column8: demonstrating high performance using only JS content. Upon deployment
column9: AdCPG identified 650 JS files from 500 domains that were not detected by any ATS filter lists and previous ATS classification tools. AdCPG plays a complementary role in identifying ATS resources while providing code-level explanations
column10: which minimizes the engineering effort required to validate ATS classification results. © 2023 Copyright held by the owner/author(s). Publication rights licensed to ACM.;code property graph ─ explainable AI ─ graph neural networks ─ web advertising ─ web tracking;;;;;

column1: Scopus;https://www.scopus.com/inward/record.uri?eid=2-s2.0-85119342184&doi=10.1145%2f3460120.3485347&partnerID=40&md5=cfae96fdb8e87450894678f1a6a0e22c;10.1145/3460120.3485347;Mitseva A. ─ Pennekamp J. ─ Lohmöller J. ─ Ziemann T. ─ Hoerchner C. ─ Wehrle K. ─ Panchenko A.;2021;27th ACM Annual Conference on Computer and Communication Security
column2: CCS 2021;Conference paper;POSTER: How Dangerous is My Click? Boosting Website Fingerprinting by Considering Sequences of Webpages;Website fingerprinting (WFP) is a special case of traffic analysis
column3: where a passive attacker infers information about the content of encrypted and anonymized connections by observing patterns of data flows. Although modern WFP attacks pose a serious threat to online privacy of users
column4: including Tor users
column5: they usually aim to detect single pages only. By ignoring the browsing behavior of users
column6: the attacker excludes valuable information: users visit multiple pages of a single website consecutively
column7: e.g.
column8: by following links. In this paper
column9: we propose two novel methods that can take advantage of the consecutive visits of multiple pages to detect websites. We show that two up to three clicks within a site allow attackers to boost the accuracy by more than 20% and to dramatically increase the threat to users' privacy. We argue that WFP defenses have to consider this new dimension of the attack surface. © 2021 Owner/Author.;traffic analysis ─ web privacy ─ website fingerprinting;;;;;

Polit.;Article;Predicting political attitudes from web tracking data: a machine learning approach;Anecdotal evidence suggests that the surge of populism and subsequent political polarization might make voters’ political preferences more detectable from digital trace data. column3: 000 website visits
column4: tracked from 1
column5: 003 users in Germany
column6: and their survey responses to explore whether website choices can accurately predict political attitudes across five dimensions: Immigration
column7: democracy

column9: populism
column10: and trust. Our findings indicate a limited ability to identify political attitudes from individuals’ website visits. Our most effective machine learning algorithm predicted interest in politics and attitudes toward democracy but with dependency on model parameters. Although website categories exhibited suggestive patterns
column11: they only marginally distinguished between individuals with anti- or pro-immigration attitudes
column12: as well as those with populist or mainstream attitudes. This further confirm the reliability of surveys in measuring attitudes compared to digital trace data and
column13: from a normative perspective

column5: IPFuture 2020;Conference paper;URL-based web tracking detection using deep learning;The pervasiveness of online web tracking poses a constant threat to the privacy of Internet users. Millions of users currently employ content-blockers in their web browsers to block tracking resources in real time. Although content-blockers are based on blacklists
column6: which are known to be difficult to maintain and easy to evade
column7: the research community has not succeeded in replacing them with better alternatives yet. Most of the methods recently proposed in the literature obtain good detection accuracy
column8: but at the expense of increasing their complexity and making them more difficult to maintain and configure by the end user. In this paper
column9: we present a new web tracking detection method
column10: called Deep Tracking Detector (DTD)
column11: that analyzes the properties of URL strings to detect tracking resources
column12: without using any other external features. Consequently
column13: DTD can easily be implemented in a browser plugin and operate in real time. Our experimental results
column14: with more than 5M HTTP requests from 100K websites
column15: show that DTD achieves a detection accuracy higher than 97% by looking only at the URL of the resources.  © 2020 IFIP.;Deep learning ─ URL classification ─ Web tracking;;;;;

column2: SVCC 2023;Conference paper;Lightweight and Effective Website Fingerprinting Over Encrypted DNS;The DNS over HTTPS (DoH) protocol is implemented to improve the original DNS protocol that uses unencrypted DNS queries and responses. With the DNS traffic
column3: an eavesdropper can easily identify websites that a user is visiting. In order to address this concern of web privacy
column4: encryption is used by performing a DNS lookup over HTTPS. In this paper
column5: we studied whether the encrypted DoH traffic could be exploited to identify websites that a user has visited. This is a different type of website fingerprinting by analyzing encrypted DNS network traffic rather than the network traffic between the client and the web server. DNS typically uses fewer network packets than a website download. Our model and algorithm can accurately predict one out of 10
column6: 000 websites with a 95% accuracy using the first 50 DoH packets. In the open-world environment with 100
column7: 000 websites
column8: our model achieves an F1-score of 93%.  © 2023 IEEE.;DNS ─ DoH ─ Privacy ─ Webbrowsing ─ Website Fingerprinting;;;;;

column1: Scopus;https://www.scopus.com/inward/record.uri?eid=2-s2.0-85088396612&doi=10.1109%2fEuroSP48549.2020.00013&partnerID=40&md5=1c4540e5149f0075f1b024cbda16288e;10.1109/EuroSP48549.2020.00013;Hu X. ─ De Tangil G.S. ─ Sastry N.;2020;5th IEEE European Symposium on Security and Privacy
column2: Euro S and P 2020;Conference paper;Multi-country Study of Third Party Trackers from Real Browser Histories;This paper aims to understand how third-party ecosystems have developed in four different countries: UK
column3: China
column4: AU
column5: US. We are interested in how wide a view a given third-party player may have
column6: of an individual user's browsing history over a period of time
column7: and of the collective browsing histories of a cohort of users in each of these countries. We study this by utilizing two complementary approaches: The first uses lists of the most popular websites per country
column8: as determined by Alexa.com. The second approach is based on the real browsing histories of a cohort of users in these countries. Our larger continuous user data collection spans over a year. Some universal patterns are seen
column9: such as more third parties on more popular websites
column10: and a specialization among trackers
column11: with some trackers present in some categories of websites but not others. However
column12: our study reveals several unexpected country-specific patterns: China has a home-grown ecosystem of third-party operators in contrast with the UK
column13: whose trackers are dominated by players hosted in the US. UK trackers are more location sensitive than Chinese trackers. One important consequence of these is that users in China are tracked lesser than users in the UK. Our unique access to the browsing patterns of a panel of users provides a realistic insight into third party exposure
column14: and suggests that studies which rely solely on Alexa top ranked websites may be over estimating the power of third parties
column15: since real users also access several niche interest sites with lesser numbers of many kinds of third parties
column16: especially advertisers. © 2020 IEEE.;browser privacy ─ cookies ─ web tracking;;;;;

column1: Scopus;https://www.scopus.com/inward/record.uri?eid=2-s2.0-85151559659&doi=10.1109%2fTIFS.2023.3262123&partnerID=40&md5=0f92de4b5fac7cfda2b33e7f6ec9e656;10.1109/TIFS.2023.3262123;Houssiau F. ─ Lienart T. ─ Hendrickx J. ─ De Montjoye Y.-A.;2023;IEEE Trans. Inf. Forensics Secur.;Article;Web Privacy: A Formal Adversarial Model for Query Obfuscation;The queries we perform
column2: the searches we make
column3: and the websites we visit-this sensitive data is collected at scale by companies as part of the services they provide. Query obfuscation
column4: intertwining the user queries with artificial queries
column5: has been proposed as a solution to protect the privacy of individuals on the web. We here present a formal model and formulate through attack models three privacy requirements for obfuscators: 1) indistinguishability
column6: that the user query should be hard to identify ─ 2) coverage
column7: that its topic should be hard to identify ─ and 3) imprecision
column8: that the query should still be hard to identify for an attacker with additional auxiliary information. The latter is needed to make the former two guarantees 'future-proof'. Using our framework
column9: we derive two important results for obfuscators. First
column10: we show that indistinguishability imposes strong bounds on the coverage and imprecision achievable by an obfuscator. Second
column11: we prove an important tradeoff between coverage and imprecision
column12: which inherently limits the strength and robustness of the privacy guarantees that an obfuscator can provide. We then introduce a family of obfuscators with provable indistinguishability guarantees
column13: which we call k- ball obfuscators
column14: and show
column15: for a range of parameter values
column16: the achievable coverage and imprecision. We show empirically that our theoretical tradeoff holds
column17: and that its bound is not tight in practice: even in a simple idealized setting
column18: there is a significant gap between practical coverage and imprecision guarantees
column19: and the optimal bounds. While obfuscators have proven popular with the general public
column20: all obfuscators currently available provide ad-hoc guarantees
column21: and have been shown to be vulnerable to attacks
column22: putting the data of users at risk. We hope this work to be a first step towards a robust evaluation of the properties of query obfuscators and the development of principled obfuscators.  © 2005-2012 IEEE.;obfuscation ─ Privacy;;;;;

column1: Scopus;https://www.scopus.com/inward/record.uri?eid=2-s2.0-85121864586&doi=10.1007%2f978-3-030-91356-4_9&partnerID=40&md5=09521a9082687eac58d7f6af4445c145;10.1007/978-3-030-91356-4_9;Akhavani S.A. ─ Jueckstock J. ─ Su J. ─ Kapravelos A. ─ Kirda E. ─ Lu L.;2021;24th International Conference on Information Security
column2: ISC 2021;Conference paper;Browserprint: an Analysis of the Impact of Browser Features on Fingerprintability and Web Privacy;Web browsers are indispensable applications in our daily lives. Millions of users use web browsers for a wide range of activities such as social media
column3: online shopping
column4: emails
column5: or surfing the web. The evolution of increasingly more complicated web applications relies on browsers constantly adding and removing features. At the same time
column6: some of these web services use browser fingerprinting to track and profile their users with clear disregard for their web privacy. In this paper
column7: we perform an empirical analysis of browser features evolution and aim to evaluate browser fingerprintability. By analyzing 33 Google Chrome
column8: 31 Mozilla Firefox
column9: and 33 Opera major browser versions released through 2016 to 2020
column10: we discover that all of these browsers have unique feature sets which makes them different from each other. By comparing these features to the fingerprinting APIs presented in literature that have appeared in this field
column11: we conclude that all of these browser versions are uniquely fingerprintable. Our results show an alarming trend that browsers are becoming more fingerprintable over time because newer versions contain more fingerprintable APIs compared to older ones. © 2021
column12: Springer Nature Switzerland AG.;Browser security ─ Fingerprinting ─ Privacy ─ Web security;;;;;

column2: WWW 2023;Conference paper;Katti: An Extensive and Scalable Tool for Website Analyses;Research on web security and privacy frequently relies on tools that analyze a set of websites. One major obstacle to the judicious analysis is the employment of a rock-solid and feature-rich web crawler. For example
column3: the automated analysis of ad-malware campaigns on websites requests crawling a vast set of domains on multiple real web browsers
column4: while simultaneously mitigating bot detections and applying user interactions on websites. Further
column5: the ability to attach various threat analysis frameworks lacks current tooling efforts in web crawling and analyses. In this paper we introduce Katti
column6: which overcomes several of today's technical hurdles in web crawling. Our tool employs a distributed task queue that efficiently and reliably handles both large crawling and threat analyses requests. Katti extensively collects all available web data through an integrated person-in-the-middle proxy. Moreover
column7: Katti is not limited to a specific use case
column8: allowing users to easily customize our tool to their individual research intends.  © 2023 Owner/Author.;analyses ─ analysis ─ crawling ─ web ─ website;;;;;

column2: ICCSA 2018;Conference paper;A browser application for keyword recommendation based on user web search;Keywords are the words and phrases that are typed into the search box of a search engine such as Google by the Internet users to find out the Web sites that match what the Internet users are trying to find. column1: Scopus;https://www.scopus.com/inward/record.uri?eid=2-s2.0-85050919130&doi=10.1145%2f3212480.3212489&partnerID=40&md5=86fd5ccb03803d2690be1a3d66285600;10.1145/3212480.3212489;Matyunin N. ─ Kolinichenko M. ─ Katzenbeisser S.;2018;11th ACM Conference on Security and Privacy in Wireless and Mobile Networks
column2: WiSec 2018;Conference paper;Tracking private browsing sessions using CPU-based covert channels;In this paper we examine the use of covert channels based on CPU load in order to achieve persistent user identification through browser sessions. In particular
column3: we demonstrate that an HTML5 video
column4: a GIF image
column5: or CSS animations on a webpage can be used to force the CPU to produce a sequence of distinct load levels
column6: even without JavaScript or any client-side code. These load levels can be then captured either by another browsing session
column7: running on the same or a different browser in parallel to the browsing session we want to identify
column8: or by a malicious app installed on the device. To get a good estimation of the CPU load caused by the target session
column9: the receiver can observe system statistics about CPU activity (app)
column10: or constantly measure time it takes to execute a known code segment (app and browser). Furthermore
column11: for mobile devices we propose a sensor-based approach to estimate the CPU load
column12: based on exploiting disturbances of the magnetometer sensor data caused by the high CPU activity. Captured loads can be decoded and translated into an identifying bit string
column13: which is transmitted back to the attacker. Due to the way loads are produced
column14: these methods are applicable even in highly restrictive browsers
column15: such as the Tor Browser
column16: and run unnoticeably to the end user. Therefore
column17: unlike existing ways of web tracking
column18: our methods circumvent most of the existing countermeasures
column19: as they store the identifying information outside the browsing session being targeted. Finally
column20: we also thoroughly evaluate and assess each presented method of generating and receiving the signal
column21: and provide an overview of potential countermeasures. © 2018 Association for Computing Machinery.;Covert channels ─ CPU ─ CSS ─ Electromagnetic emanations ─ HTML5 ─ JavaScript ─ Tor ─ User identification ─ Web tracking;;;;;

column1: Scopus;https://www.scopus.com/inward/record.uri?eid=2-s2.0-85077988196&doi=10.1109%2fDSC47296.2019.8937592&partnerID=40&md5=a5a309c35e22c83b91ce36bffea81352;10.1109/DSC47296.2019.8937592;Lin C.-H. ─ Lee S.-H. ─ Huang H.-C. ─ Wang C.-W. ─ Hsu C.-W. ─ Shieh S.;2019;3rd IEEE Conference on Dependable and Secure Computing
column2: DSC 2019;Conference paper;DT-Track: Using DNS-Timing Side Channel for Mobile User Tracking;Targeted advertising is a form of online advertising that is directed towards audiences with certain traits. User tracking is useful to provide tailored web content or online advertisement. However
column3: in the case without user online identities
column4: targeted advertising becomes difficult. The challenge gets even higher in a mobile environment as the mobile user roams from one location to another location. In this paper

column8: which are commonly used. © 2019 IEEE.;DNS side channel ─ user identification ─ Web tracking;;;;;

column1: Scopus;https://www.scopus.com/inward/record.uri?eid=2-s2.0-85053133063&doi=10.1109%2fCITS.2018.8440163&partnerID=40&md5=332096ccaa4cf8e4abc0088b5bd402a0;10.1109/CITS.2018.8440163;Elbanna A. ─ Abdelbaki N.;2018;2018 International Conference on Computer
column2: Information and Telecommunication Systems
column3: CITS 2018;Conference paper;Browsers fingerprinting motives
column4: methods
column5: and countermeasures;With the continuous and aggressive competition in advertising businesses
column6: uncontrollably desires have emerged to identify and classify consumers. It is proven that companies must have a clear definition of its target market. Based on this we have seen different ways to identify
column7: analyze
column8: and track consumers
column9: either voluntarily or without their consent. Browser fingerprinting techniques have evolved from being privacy-friendly to privacy intrusive to serve these demands. In this paper we introduce different browser fingerprinting techniques and their relevant technologies in the wild. It additionally elaborates widely known countermeasures. © 2018 IEEE.;browser fingerprinting countermeasures and implications ─ browser fingerprinting techniques ─ Web tracking;;;;;

column1: Scopus;https://www.scopus.com/inward/record.uri?eid=2-s2.0-85006932857&doi=10.1109%2fCCECE.2016.7726741&partnerID=40&md5=8c4c57f70b316e76d0f840f6a27e7294;10.1109/CCECE.2016.7726741;Hamed A. ─ Ben Ayed H.K.;2016;2016 IEEE Canadian Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering
column2: CCECE 2016;Conference paper;Privacy risk assessment for Web tracking: A user-oriented approach toward privacy risk assessment for Web tracking;Web tracking raises a privacy issue since users' browsing activities and personal information are collected and revealed to third parties without their consent. column3: we present a dynamic privacy scoring model that assesses the risk to users' privacy associated to tracking components. This model is based on two parameters

column1: Scopus;https://www.scopus.com/inward/record.uri?eid=2-s2.0-84959542649&doi=10.1145%2f2808138.2808140&partnerID=40&md5=9ce9c2750dadbdd8d1dc6c344ced17a1;10.1145/2808138.2808140;Aleyasen A. ─ Starov O. ─ Au A.P. ─ Schiffman A. ─ Shrager J.;2015;ACM 14th Workshop on Privacy in the Electronic Society
column2: WPES 2015;Conference paper;On the privacy practices of Just Plain Sites;"In addition to visiting popular sites such as Facebook and Google
column3: web users often visit more modest sites
column4: such as those operated by bloggers
column5: or by local organizations such as schools. Such sites
column6: which we call ""Just Plain Sites"" (JPSs)
column7: are likely to inadvertently present greater privacy risks than highly popular sites
column8: because they are unable to afford privacy expertise. To assess the prevalence of the privacy risks to which JPSs may inadvertently be exposing their visitors
column9: we examined privacy practices that could be observed by analysis of JPS landing pages. We found that many JPSs collect a great deal of information from their visitors
column10: and share a great deal of information about their visitors with third parties. For example
column11: we found that an average of 7 third party organizations are informed when a user visits a JPS. Many JPSs additionally permit a great deal of tracking of their visitors. For example
column12: we found that third party cookies are used by more than 50% of JPSs. We also found that many JPSs use deprecated or unsafe security practices. Our goal is not to scold JPS operators
column13: but to raise awareness of these facts among both JPS operators and visitors
column14: possibly encouraging operators to take greater care in their implementations
column15: and visitors to take greater care in how

column2: M and N 2017;Conference paper;Towards accurate detection of obfuscated web tracking;Web tracking is currently recognized as one of the most important privacy threats on the Internet. Over the last years
